item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image in the article appears to be from here, with no indication that it has been released under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the image has been removed. A replacement image will be added shortly.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent series of edits by Theosis was reverted, as it introduced new content without authorial or staff permission. This would be considered a major edit, which per Site Rules, is not allowed without permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this article until the very end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have such a weak spot for cosmic/interstellar entities-scp. Updoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized this is kinda like an Opposite-Hateful Star. I like it better than the hateful star though.

My masters. Oh, my masters.
We are coming home.

That got a lotta emotion out of me. Dunno what exactly, but I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has anyone considered that the masters could be the Bigfoots rather than humans?

Edit: Wait, someone already said this. Still, thing to consider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made an account so I could second this. Stages 7, 8, and the two paragraphs immediately after have some hefty blocks of duplicated sentences. How objectionable would it be for me to tidy those up? The author doesn't show any activity since 2012.

item.title: Brilliant!
item.content:encoded:
Regardless of how well you think you know the wiki, there will always be hidden gems you did not know about yet for you to discover. This article is one of these gems. It is a shame that it seems to be so unknown.
The implications of this article are brilliant. It has everything a good SCP needs.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do love this but -

Towards the end of Step 7: "The arrival of the inhabitants, is, however, doubted by the Foundation, as evidenced by the fact that after the SCP-1795 leaves, the structures will gradually fall into disrepair and ruin."

And then after Step 8: "Their eventual arrival is doubted by the Foundation, since, as demonstrated by the case of SCP-1795-██, the structures will gradually fall into disrepair and ruin throughout the decades following the departure of the SCP-1795 responsible for the world."

Was this intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was one of my early favorites and rereading it now, it still holds up. I will gladly keep pimping/cross-linking this until the sun goes out. Astronaut Joe, wherever you are, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should read the halo forerunner series Greg bear

item.title: Random Article Review #5, 11 October 2016
item.content:encoded:
A good sampling from a period in site history where things took a noticeable turn towards science fiction. The length of the article serves in this case to make for an immersive reading experience, although the middle section describing in exacting detail the planet creation process does drag a bit.

The larger implications of the article act as a nice kicker, although I might have gone a different route to tell the story than the dueling memos device. For such a ponderous article, the last act is sort of light in the storytelling department, and it detracts a bit from the epic scale of what's being attempted here.

A solid entry nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I was going to neutral vote for one key reason: I really don't like the idea of SCPs that explain natural phenomena. That's what this was looking like it was going to turn out to be. "This is how planets are made!" Thankfully, it didn't turn into that.

And it (I think) is the first article to introduce the idea of a pattern screamer (which is an awesome name, by the way), so that's a plus. I think the transmission was a little shoehorned in at the end, but that's… okay. Basically, I upvoted, but I only thought it was good. I guess it's just hard to suspend my disbelief for this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do you mean? They love us! What could possibly go wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'We are coming home.'

Well, sounds like we dun goofed… lucky we have a few million years to sort things out first…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this thing. They're so big and so strange and so old that we have no possible model for how they can exist, much less function… our only guesses about where they come from are derived from what they do, instead of how they're born or how they age… and rather than menacing us, they call us mommy.

Also, I came here to find out whether the blackboxed observational SCP object was always intended to be SCP-2154, and instead discovered that they apparently came about completely independently. So that's interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, it would fit nicely with my own scip, [[SCP-2004]].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea behind it, but I'm kind of so-so on being able to document the process by which these things make planets in such high detail. Like I get that the researchers are using anomalous methods to do so, but for me there's an appeal in spooky space shit that's so distant from us that we don't have the technology to really discover everything we want to know about it, it makes it seem much more alien.

I think it'd be more effective if the foundation was clearly struggling to even make out quite what was going on. Being able to see that things are being constructed by giant spider constructs that cut their own heads off when they're no longer useful, being able to identify that a statue on a planet light years away is human in shape, these things just drag it down for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it but I noticed a wording error that bugs me.
Twice the author uses 'conductive' referring to habitation by humanoids while clearly meaning 'conducive'.
Fixing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First it's all "we're not sure if it's sapient or not", but then it's all "the spider things are slaved to its sapience", and then it repeats the "disrepair and ruin" line.

The arrival of the inhabitants, is, however, doubted by the Foundation, as evidenced by the fact that after the SCP-1795 leaves, the structures will gradually fall into disrepair and ruin.

Their eventual arrival is doubted by the Foundation, since, as demonstrated by the case of SCP-1795-██, the structures will gradually fall into disrepair and ruin throughout the decades following the departure of the SCP-1795 responsible for the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone has a thing with whales. I mistakenly thought that someone was you. Maybe it's Djoric.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
Discussion of 231 is touchy enough as it is on it's own discussion page, there's no reason to let it bleed into other article discussion pages.

Also, a closed staff post means "Do not reply unless you are staff". Please do not reply to closed staff posts in the future.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded: Stirring up trouble? I don't quite understand…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh? I have no particular connection to whales. There's a semi-joking theory (possibly Djoric's to begin with) that SCP-231-7 is actually a whale. I was referencing it in my earlier post because I find the theory amusing. I don't believe I refer to them in any of my SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Leonard, but I have to ask you a question: What's with you and whales?

item.title: STAFF POST - CLOSED
item.content:encoded:
This conversation is not really about SCP-1795 anymore, and I'm not seeing a reason to bring up SCP-231 except perhaps to stir up trouble.

This sub-thread is now closed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Write the tale!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, this almost reads like there could be a connection between this and SCP-962.

This is quite good, and feels nicely mythic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
She's not an engineered organism, she's a regular human (or perhaps whale) who has had something done to her. Moreover, it's not what was done to her that makes her miserable, it's the treatment of the Foundation that makes her suffer. These things almost certainly didn't start out as human.

I'm really not sure why you're pushing this issue. These are very inhuman entities, and it's reasonable to expect that they would therefore have a mindset different from that of the average human. Plainly, they haven't gone mad with isolation and boredom, so it's kinda silly to claim that it must be horrible to be them. There's certainly anguish in the message, but that's the anguish of believing something cherished and loved to be lost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would it matter if it is an "engineered organism"? SCP-231-7 is technically "engineered", since she is controlled by human-made Satanic rituals, and her existance is downright miserable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One more patient than you?

Enjoyment is in large part a result of our biology. Joy is a reward mechanism in our brains. We take satisfaction in helping others because altruism is a survival trait. We enjoy certain foods more than others because they contain nutrients that were hard to find in the environment we evolved in. We find constant stimulation appealing because we're social animals. Etc. We enjoy what we enjoy because it's correlated with being an effective human likely to pass on our genes. (Note, however, that this is independent with any normative ethical claims.)

So for something that's developed in wholly different environment from ours, it's probably going to find enjoyment in the sorts of behaviors that are successful in that environment. This is doubly true if its an engineered organism — why make servants that hate their jobs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A non-human entity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What kind of entity would find billions of years of floating around enjoyable?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So? I mean, why should everything with human-level intelligence find the same things enjoyable? Hell, there's a huge range amongst what humans find enjoyable. Just because you yourself wouldn't care for something doesn't mean that no intelligent entity would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clams do not have the mental capacity of humans, though. These guys are at least at the level of human inteligence, seeing how they make planets and buildings. Let me sse if I can site anything.

It is unknown whether cases of SCP-1795 are sapient. Although they are capable of highly complex tasks, outlined below, it is unknown whether they are aware of, control, or self-regulate these actions, or if they operate on little more than animalistic instinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would it be horrible for them? Sure, you might not much relish floating out in space making planets non-stop, but you're human. You wouldn't relish remaining stuck to a rock filtering plankton out of seawater for your whole life, but I hear clams are quite happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This is an awesome article. Upvoted. Thinking more deeply, you begin to realize that SCP-1795-1 is in a constant state of misery, doomed to float around the galaxy for billions of years, fully conscious, never eating, never resting, never sleeping, and worst of all, never dying. Some of these guys probably fly into stars for the sole purpose of ending their misery. But, others realize their duty, and create planets, only for those planets to never be inhabited.
What if Earth was made by these creatures? It would make sense. Earth being the one planet that protects the rest of the universe, (See SCP-2001 ) we would be pretty important to these guys.

There's a reason behind SCP-2001, and it's nowhere near as terrifying as all of my colleagues think. Space wants to protect us. There are things out there that would wipe humanity clean off of this plane of existence, and we are too precious to our mother, to our host, to the universe we live in, for her to allow that to happen.

Perhaps SCP-1795-1 did not listen to the universes warning, and this eternal life of wandering is their punishment.

But, that's just a theory. A GAME THEORY! Actually, no. We're not going there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept, interesting backstory, excellent execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can imagine these things going all around the galaxy, building new instances of themselves by plunging into nebulae and then parting, looking for protostars.

Although it's an interesting idea, I have several doubts about the whole concept. What happens with all the protostar material during and after the production of the new planet? Does it interact in any way with the surface? How long the new manufactured planets are exposed to those materials in the star system? Also, the greatest anomalous capacity of these things is not that they seem to be able to plan their moves ahead, or that they are able to manipulate matter to an insanely degree of precision. My problem with them is that they are huge, plain an simple. No coherent organism or machine of that size would resist the forces involved in its function; I can imagine them as massive clouds of matter, kept ¡n a coherent shape by some force, or unending groups of drones that look like clouds. Talking about massive, do they have any gravity of their own? In any other case, these world wombs are structurally impossible. Yeah, I know, just another anomaly to note in the report. That said, I loved that they were subluminal! : D

Also, I'd like to know what kind of a jerk makes Magrathean factory floors and lets them loose upon a galaxy. How many previously inhabited planets may the hearts have encountered while traversing space? And how many protocivilizations these worlds housed, and then got destroyed by stages 5 and 6?

Anyhow, neat. +1, and I want to know more about the SCPs involved in space exploration that were used to discover and study this thing. No, for real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure it's scary and such and the implications are ripe for hard science fiction but I just don't like how easy it seems for these things to make shiny Earth planets, and how insanely numerous they are. I want to love it and I feel this inexplicable reaction after reading it of pure giddiness, love, and already leaping ahead with What-If? scenarios but when I actually start thinking about the logistics involved and the persistent convenience of its mechanisms, I get turned off.

Also I don't see how this ties in with SCP-1678, which it appears to try to tie in with. If Max Headroom were here, he could make it all work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely terrific in every way. Very easy to picture, a great idea, and it just kept building and building towards the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is freaking awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, why haven't I read this before? How coincidental that I just read about GOI "Pattern Screamer".

I'll gladly upvote this, with my invincible suspension of belief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a wonderful, what an under appreciated SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It will then proceed to create a planet.

I for some reason loved this bit. Considering how large this is as a process, and its so simply put made me laugh a bit. Though at the same time perhaps this could be slightly restructured.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was on the fence until the last addendum. To wit, "Sometimes the horror isn't the SCP; it's being the SCP."

TAKE THIS! MY LOVE, MY anger, AND ALL OF MY +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A-freaking-mazing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I reached the last addendum, I couldn't upvote harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When this was first posted I speculated they were created by SCP-1000, since they were known to build biotechnology beyond ours…

I am totally on board with this.

item.title: Blackboxes
item.content:encoded: Could this be the result of long-term ocular studies of SCP-134?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great read, upvoted.

By the way, this sentence.

I have re-submitted Document U1795 for your consideration.

Is it suppose to be type of J1795?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I presumed the foundation likely uses a number of SCP's to scan space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know about anybody else, but this bit: "It is worth noting that throughout the Foundation’s ████ ███████ year-long study of cases of SCP-1795…" really bugs me. From the amount of characters blackboxed, I'm guessing it's in the order of millions or billions of years, which seems incredibly improbable to me.

Of course, if that relates to "[harnessing] the latent temporal effects of SCP-███ has allowed the peliminary observation of instances of SCP-1795 over the course of the past fifteen million years" then the first "number" is inconsistent.

Also: "The second property is far more readily observable, and understood to a far greater decree."
I'm sure you mean "degree" instead of "decree." :P

"The protoplanet will begin showing signs magma eruptions…"
Missing an "of" there I think.

Also, how did the Foundation discover them? They're "impossible [to observe] with current telescope technology" so how would they have known they exist without outside information? There's nothing I can find regarding this anywhere in the article.

I apologize if any of this has been mentioned before, I only skimmed the discussion. Either way , it's a very interesting article, and I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I'd also like to see this angle explored too.
Anyways, lovin this already, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…maybe… maybe OUR world was created by them, too.
We just don' t remember.
I know, this hypothesis needs some sort of explanation to why there was evolution and all that stuff and we didn' t ''start'' as humans just like we are now.
But that' s not the point.

The point would be that we almost got exterminated.
Exterminated by enemy beings.
Beings that are still out there.

Waiting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it and I definitely upvote it simply for being a decent space SCP. It still could use some work though.

As Drewbear already said, drop SCP-███'s ability to view the creatures/factories through time. You already said there are one to fifty billion of the things in our galaxy. There is no need to use such cheap plot-hole-insulation and frankly I found myself more interested in SCP-███ than SCP-1795 for a while there.

Another big deal breaker is the last transmission-thing.
First thing first: the transmission was an audio message. How did we receive this audio message? Did SCP-1795 sort of broadcast it using radio waves at SCP-███? If so - how did the Foundation decode the message into an audio file? Are those ancient, humongous, inter-stellar creatures using modern-day Earth's radio broadcasting protocols? That's just stupid.

Next, if we're ignoring the whole voice issue and just go with it, the voice is speaking in some ancient presumably dead language, right?

Language was virtually identical to ancient ██████ dialect.

Now, the actual language it never directly given, but let's just assume it's proto-Latin or whatever.
What is the proto-Latin word for "galaxy"?

…But we never dared to hope that some would remain in [STATIC] part of [STATIC] galaxy.

The final problem I have with this SCP is the fact it has a 'top' and a 'bottom'. Why would it have a 'top' and a 'bottom'? There is no up or down in space, and the object has no distinct shape.

All of these complaints are nitpicking though, and, at in my opinion, do not hurt the overall quality of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it! A much better Precursors/Creators story than Prometheus! I am intrigued as the hidden SCPs which allow the Foundation to explore 1795 in such detail. I would hope that use of the item comes at a terrible cost if anyone fancies taking up the writing challenge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EARTH… FIRE… WIND… WATER… HEART!

BY YOUR POWERS COMBINED I AM CAPTAIN PLANET!

I'm just saying it's horrifically convenient this SCP is heart shaped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course you can!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When this was first posted I speculated they were created by SCP-1000, since they were known to build biotechnology beyond ours…
